function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content:
Clone and thoroughly analyze the GSD v2 repository at https://github.com/gsd-build/gsd-2.git. Read EVERY file: README, all source code, config files, templates, docs. I need a comprehensive understanding of:

1. What GSD v2 is and how it differs from v1 (v1 was a Claude Code plugin with 8 stages: IDEA->DISCUSS->RESEARCH->PLAN->APPROVE->EXECUTE->VERIFY->SHIP)
2. The full architecture: how projects are structured, how tasks flow, how agents are coordinated
3. The lifecycle/phase model — what stages exist and how transitions work
4. Task formats, plan formats, state management
5. How it dispatches work to agents
6. CLI interface and commands
7. Integration points (MCP, hooks, etc.)
8. What's genuinely new vs v1

Compare it explicitly to:
- GSD v1 (IDEA->DISCUSS->RESEARCH->PLAN->APPROVE->EXECUTE->VERIFY->SHIP, PLAN.md with XML tasks in waves, dispatches to Paperclip/OpenClaw/Hermes)
- ClawTeam harness (DISCUSS->PLAN->EXECUTE->VERIFY->SHIP, sprint contracts, tmux spawning, git worktrees)
- The other frameworks in the multi-agent landscape (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph, MetaGPT, Swarm)

Provide a detailed technical summary with specific code references."
<parameter name="context">The user (Aaron Whaley) built a custom GSD v1 system for his legal automation stack. It's a Node.js library at /opt/data/gsd-lawyerinc that manages project lifecycles. He's evaluating whether to adopt GSD v2, merge it with ClawTeam concepts, or build something new. The GSD v2 repo is from the original GSD creator.

{"content": "     1|# What is GSD?
     2|
     3|GSD is an AI-powered development agent that turns project ideas into working software. Describe what you want to build, and GSD researches, plans, codes, tests, and commits — with clean git history and full cost tracking.
     4|
     5|## How It Works
     6|
     7|GSD breaks your project into manageable pieces and works through them systematically:
     8|
     9|```
    10|You describe your project
    11|    ↓
    12|GSD creates a milestone with slices (features)
    13|    ↓
    14|Each slice is decomposed into tasks
    15|    ↓
    16|Tasks are executed one at a time in fresh AI sessions
    17|    ↓
    18|Code is committed, verified, and the next task begins
    19|```
    20|
    21|You can stay hands-on with **step mode** (reviewing each step) or let GSD run autonomously with **auto mode** while you grab coffee.
    22|
    23|## Key Features
    24|
    25|- **Autonomous execution** — `/gsd auto` runs research, planning, coding, testing, and committing without intervention
    26|- **20+ LLM providers** — Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, OpenRouter, GitHub Copilot, Amazon Bedrock, local models, and more
    27|- **Git isolation** — Each milestone works in its own worktree branch, merged cleanly when done
    28|- **Cost tracking** — Real-time token usage, budget ceilings, and automatic model downgrading
    29|- **Crash recovery** — Sessions resume automatically after interruptions
    30|- **Skills system** — Domain-specific instruction sets for frameworks, languages, and tools
    31|- **Parallel milestones** — Run multiple milestones simultaneously in isolated worktrees
    32|- **Remote questions** — Get Discord, Slack, or Telegram notifications when GSD needs input
    33|- **Web interface** — Browser-based dashboard with real-time progress
    34|- **VS Code extension** — Chat participant, sidebar dashboard, and full command palette
    35|- **Headless mode** — Run in CI pipelines, cron jobs, and scripted automation
    36|
    37|## Quick Start
    38|
    39|```bash
    40|# Install
    41|npm install -g gsd-pi
    42|
    43|# Launch
    44|gsd
    45|
    46|# Start autonomous mode
    47|/gsd auto
    48|```
    49|
    50|See [Installation](getting-started/installation.md) for detailed setup instructions.
    51|
    52|## Two Ways to Work
    53|
    54|| Mode | Command | Best For |
    55||------|---------|----------|
    56|| **Step** | `/gsd` | Staying in the loop, reviewing each step |
    57|| **Auto** | `/gsd auto` | Walking away, overnight builds, batch work |
    58|
    59|The recommended workflow: run auto mode in one terminal, steer from another. See [Step Mode](core-concepts/step-mode.md) and [Auto Mode](core-concepts/auto-mode.md).
    60|
    61|## Requirements
    62|
    63|- **Node.js** 22.0.0 or later (24 LTS recommended)
    64|- **Git** installed and configured
    65|- An API key for at least one LLM provider (or use browser sign-in for Anthropic/GitHub Copilot)
    66|", "total_lines": 65, "file_size": 2570, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# How GSD Organizes Work
     2|
     3|GSD uses a three-level hierarchy to break projects into manageable pieces that an AI can execute reliably.
     4|
     5|## The Hierarchy
     6|
     7|```
     8|Milestone  →  a shippable version (4-10 slices)
     9|  Slice    →  one demoable vertical feature (1-7 tasks)
    10|    Task   →  one context-window-sized unit of work
    11|```
    12|
    13|### Milestones
    14|
    15|A milestone is a shippable version of your project — an MVP, a major release, or a feature set that delivers standalone value. Milestones typically contain 4-10 slices.
    16|
    17|Examples:
    18|- "MVP with user auth, dashboard, and settings"
    19|- "v2.0 with real-time collaboration and API v2"
    20|- "Security hardening milestone"
    21|
    22|### Slices
    23|
    24|A slice is one demoable, vertical capability within a milestone. It cuts across layers (database, backend, frontend) to deliver something you could show to a user. Slices contain 1-7 tasks.
    25|
    26|Examples:
    27|- "User authentication with JWT"
    28|- "Dashboard layout with charts"
    29|- "API rate limiting"
    30|
    31|### Tasks
    32|
    33|A task is the smallest unit of work — something that fits in one AI context window. If a task can't be completed in a single AI session, it's broken into smaller tasks.
    34|
    35|Examples:
    36|- "Create the User model and migration"
    37|- "Implement JWT middleware"
    38|- "Build the login form component"
    39|
    40|## The `.gsd/` Directory
    41|
    42|All project state lives on disk in a `.gsd/` directory at your project root:
    43|
    44|```
    45|.gsd/
    46|  PROJECT.md          — living description of what the project is
    47|  REQUIREMENTS.md     — requirement contract (active/validated/deferred)
    48|  DECISIONS.md        — append-only architectural decisions log
    49|  KNOWLEDGE.md        — cross-session rules, patterns, and lessons
    50|  RUNTIME.md          — runtime context: API endpoints, env vars, services
    51|  STATE.md            — quick-glance status of current work
    52|  PREFERENCES.md      — project-level preferences (optional)
    53|  milestones/
    54|    M001/
    55|      M001-ROADMAP.md — slice plan with risk levels and dependencies
    56|      M001-CONTEXT.md — scope and goals from discussion phase
    57|      slices/
    58|        S01/
    59|          S01-PLAN.md     — task decomposition for this slice
    60|          S01-SUMMARY.md  — what was built and what changed
    61|          S01-UAT.md      — human test script
    62|          tasks/
    63|            T01-PLAN.md   — detailed plan for this task
    64|            T01-SUMMARY.md — what the task accomplished
    65|```
    66|
    67|### Key Files
    68|
    69|| File | Purpose |
    70||------|---------|
    71|| `PROJECT.md` | High-level project description, updated as the project evolves |
    72|| `REQUIREMENTS.md` | Formal requirement contract — tracks what's active, validated, and deferred |
    73|| `DECISIONS.md` | Append-only log of architectural decisions with rationale |
    74|| `KNOWLEDGE.md` | Rules, patterns, and lessons learned across sessions — GSD reads this at the start of every task |
    75|| `RUNTIME.md` | Runtime context like API URLs, ports, and environment variables |
    76|| `STATE.md` | Current status at a glance — auto-generated, don't edit manually |
    77|
    78|## How Work Flows
    79|
    80|Each slice flows through phases:
    81|
    82|```
    83|Plan → Execute (per task) → Complete → Reassess Roadmap → Next Slice
    84|```
    85|
    86|1. **Plan** — GSD scouts the codebase, researches relevant docs, and decomposes the slice into tasks with clear requirements
    87|2. **Execute** — Each task runs in a fresh AI session with focused context
    88|3. **Complete** — GSD writes summaries, generates a UAT script, and commits
    89|4. **Reassess** — The roadmap is checked against reality — slices may be reordered, added, or removed
    90|5. **Next Slice** — The loop continues until all slices are done
    91|
    92|After all slices complete, a **milestone validation** gate checks that success criteria were actually met before sealing the milestone.
    93|
    94|## Adding Knowledge
    95|
    96|GSD maintains a knowledge base that persists across sessions. Add rules, patterns, or lessons:
    97|
    98|```
    99|/gsd knowledge rule "Always use parameterized queries for database access"
   100|/gsd knowledge pattern "Service classes go in src/services/"
   101|/gsd knowledge lesson "The OAuth flow requires the redirect URL to match exactly"
   102|```
   103|
   104|This knowledge is injected into every task prompt automatically.
   105|", "total_lines": 104, "file_size": 4089, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Auto Mode
     2|
     3|Auto mode is GSD's autonomous execution engine. Run `/gsd auto`, walk away, come back to built software with clean git history.
     4|
     5|## Starting Auto Mode
     6|
     7|```
     8|/gsd auto
     9|```
    10|
    11|GSD reads `.gsd/STATE.md`, determines the next unit of work, creates a fresh AI session with all relevant context, and lets the AI execute. When it finishes, GSD reads disk state again and dispatches the next unit. This continues until the milestone is complete.
    12|
    13|## The Execution Loop
    14|
    15|Each slice flows through phases automatically:
    16|
    17|```
    18|Plan → Execute (per task) → Complete → Reassess Roadmap → Next Slice
    19|                                                           ↓ (all done)
    20|                                                   Validate Milestone
    21|```
    22|
    23|- **Plan** — scouts the codebase, researches docs, decomposes the slice into tasks
    24|- **Execute** — runs each task in a fresh context window
    25|- **Complete** — writes summary, UAT script, marks roadmap, commits
    26|- **Reassess** — checks if the roadmap still makes sense after what was learned
    27|- **Validate** — after all slices, verifies success criteria were actually met
    28|
    29|## Controlling Auto Mode
    30|
    31|### Pause
    32|
    33|Press **Escape**. The conversation is preserved. You can interact with the agent, inspect state, or resume.
    34|
    35|### Resume
    36|
    37|```
    38|/gsd auto
    39|```
    40|
    41|Auto mode reads disk state and picks up where it left off.
    42|
    43|### Stop
    44|
    45|```
    46|/gsd stop
    47|```
    48|
    49|Stops auto mode gracefully. Can be run from a different terminal.
    50|
    51|### Steer
    52|
    53|```
    54|/gsd steer
    55|```
    56|
    57|Modify plan documents during execution without stopping. Changes are picked up at the next phase boundary.
    58|
    59|### Capture Thoughts
    60|
    61|```
    62|/gsd capture "add rate limiting to API endpoints"
    63|```
    64|
    65|Fire-and-forget thought capture. Captures are triaged automatically between tasks without pausing execution. See [Captures & Triage](../features/captures.md).
    66|
    67|## Fresh Session Per Unit
    68|
    69|Every task gets a clean AI context window. No accumulated garbage, no quality degradation from context bloat. The dispatch prompt includes everything needed — task plans, prior summaries, decisions, dependency context — so the AI starts oriented.
    70|
    71|## Git Isolation
    72|
    73|GSD isolates milestone work using one of three modes:
    74|
    75|| Mode | How It Works | Best For |
    76||------|-------------|----------|
    77|| `worktree` (default) | Each milestone gets its own directory and branch | Most projects |
    78|| `branch` | Work happens in the project root on a milestone branch | Submodule-heavy repos |
    79|| `none` | Work happens directly on your current branch | Hot-reload workflows |
    80|
    81|In worktree mode, all commits are squash-merged to main as one clean commit when the milestone completes. See [Git & Worktrees](../configuration/git-settings.md).
    82|
    83|## Crash Recovery
    84|
    85|If a session dies, the next `/gsd auto` reads the surviving session file, synthesizes a recovery briefing from every tool call that made it to disk, and resumes with full context.
    86|
    87|In headless mode (`gsd headless auto`), crashes trigger automatic restart with exponential backoff (5s → 10s → 30s, up to 3 attempts). Combined with crash recovery, this enables true overnight "fire and forget" execution.
    88|
    89|## Provider Error Recovery
    90|
    91|GSD handles provider errors automatically:
    92|
    93|| Error Type | Examples | What Happens |
    94||-----------|----------|-------------|
    95|| Rate limit | 429, "too many requests" | Auto-resumes after cooldown (60s or retry-after header) |
    96|| Server error | 500, 502, 503, "overloaded" | Auto-resumes after 30s |
    97|| Permanent | "unauthorized", "invalid key" | Pauses — requires manual resume |
    98|
    99|No manual intervention needed for transient errors.
   100|
   101|## Timeout Supervision
   102|
   103|Three timeout tiers prevent runaway sessions:
   104|
   105|| Timeout | Default | What Happens |
   106||---------|---------|-------------|
   107|| Soft | 20 min | Warns the AI to wrap up |
   108|| Idle | 10 min | Detects stalls, intervenes |
   109|| Hard | 30 min | Pauses auto mode |
   110|
   111|Configure in preferences:
   112|
   113|```yaml
   114|auto_supervisor:
   115|  soft_timeout_minutes: 20
   116|  idle_timeout_minutes: 10
   117|  hard_timeout_minutes: 30
   118|```
   119|
   120|## Verification Gates
   121|
   122|Configure shell commands that run automatically after every task:
   123|
   124|```yaml
   125|verification_commands:
   126|  - npm run lint
   127|  - npm run test
   128|verification_auto_fix: true    # auto-retry on failure
   129|verification_max_retries: 2    # max retry attempts
   130|```
   131|
   132|If verification fails, the AI sees the output and attempts to fix the issues before advancing. This ensures quality gates are enforced mechanically.
   133|
   134|## Slice Discussion Gate
   135|
   136|For projects requiring human review before each slice:
   137|
   138|```yaml
   139|require_slice_discussion: true
   140|```
   141|
   142|Auto mode pauses before each slice, showing the plan for your approval before building.
   143|
   144|## Stuck Detection
   145|
   146|GSD uses sliding-window analysis to detect stuck loops — not just "same unit dispatched twice" but also cycles like A→B→A→B. On detection, GSD retries once with a diagnostic prompt. If it fails again, auto mode stops with details so you can intervene.
   147|
   148|## Cost Tracking
   149|
   150|Every unit's token usage and cost is captured, broken down by phase, slice, and model. The dashboard shows running totals and projections. Budget ceilings can pause auto mode before overspending. See [Cost Management](../features/cost-management.md).
   151|
   152|## Dashboard
   153|
   154|`Ctrl+Alt+G` or `/gsd status` shows real-time progress:
   155|
   156|- Current milestone, slice, and task
   157|- Auto mode elapsed time and phase
   158|- Per-unit cost and token breakdown
   159|- Cost projections
   160|- Completed and in-progress units
   161|- Pending capture count
   162|- Parallel worker status (when running parallel milestones)
   163|
   164|## HTML Reports
   165|
   166|After a milestone completes, GSD generates a self-contained HTML report in `.gsd/reports/` with project summary, progress tree, dependency graph, cost metrics, timeline, and changelog. Generate manually with:
   167|
   168|```
   169|/gsd export --html
   170|/gsd export --html --all    # all milestones
   171|```
   172|
   173|## Diagnostic Tools
   174|
   175|If auto mode has issues, GSD provides two diagnostic tools:
   176|
   177|- **`/gsd doctor`** — validates `.gsd/` integrity, checks referential consistency, fixes structural issues
   178|- **`/gsd forensics`** — full post-mortem debugger with anomaly detection, unit traces, metrics analysis, and AI-guided investigation
   179|
   180|```
   181|/gsd doctor
   182|/gsd forensics [optional problem description]
   183|```
   184|", "total_lines": 183, "file_size": 6185, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Step Mode
     2|
     3|Step mode is GSD's interactive, one-step-at-a-time workflow. You stay in the loop, reviewing output between each step.
     4|
     5|## Starting Step Mode
     6|
     7|```
     8|/gsd
     9|```
    10|
    11|GSD reads the state of your `.gsd/` directory and presents a wizard showing what's completed and what's next. It then executes one unit of work and pauses.
    12|
    13|## How It Works
    14|
    15|Step mode adapts to your project's current state:
    16|
    17|| State | What Happens |
    18||-------|-------------|
    19|| No `.gsd/` directory | Starts a discussion flow to capture your project vision |
    20|| Milestone exists, no roadmap | Opens a discussion or research phase for the milestone |
    21|| Roadmap exists, slices pending | Plans the next slice or executes the next task |
    22|| Mid-task | Resumes where you left off |
    23|
    24|After each unit completes, you see results and decide what to do next. This is ideal for:
    25|
    26|- New projects where you want to shape the architecture
    27|- Critical work where you want to review each step
    28|- Learning how GSD works before trusting auto mode
    29|
    30|## Steering During Step Mode
    31|
    32|Between steps, you can:
    33|
    34|- **Discuss** — `/gsd discuss` to talk through architecture decisions
    35|- **Skip** — `/gsd skip` to prevent a unit from being dispatched
    36|- **Undo** — `/gsd undo` to revert the last completed unit
    37|- **Switch to auto** — `/gsd auto` to let GSD continue autonomously
    38|
    39|## When to Use Step Mode
    40|
    41|- **First milestone** — Review GSD's work before trusting it to run solo
    42|- **Architectural decisions** — When you want to guide the approach
    43|- **Unfamiliar codebases** — When you want to ensure GSD understands the project
    44|- **High-stakes changes** — When mistakes would be costly
    45|
    46|## Transitioning to Auto Mode
    47|
    48|Once you're comfortable with GSD's approach, switch to auto mode:
    49|
    50|```
    51|/gsd auto
    52|```
    53|
    54|You can always press **Escape** to pause auto mode and return to step-by-step control.
    55|", "total_lines": 54, "file_size": 1839, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Commands
     2|
     3|## Session Commands
     4|
     5|| Command | Description |
     6||---------|-------------|
     7|| `/gsd` | Step mode — execute one unit at a time |
     8|| `/gsd auto` | Autonomous mode — research, plan, execute, commit, repeat |
     9|| `/gsd quick` | Quick task with GSD guarantees but no full planning |
    10|| `/gsd stop` | Stop auto mode gracefully |
    11|| `/gsd pause` | Pause auto mode (preserves state) |
    12|| `/gsd steer` | Modify plan documents during execution |
    13|| `/gsd discuss` | Discuss architecture and decisions |
    14|| `/gsd status` | Progress dashboard |
    15|| `/gsd widget` | Cycle dashboard widget: full / small / min / off |
    16|| `/gsd queue` | Queue and reorder future milestones |
    17|| `/gsd capture` | Fire-and-forget thought capture |
    18|| `/gsd triage` | Manually trigger capture triage |
    19|| `/gsd dispatch` | Dispatch a specific phase directly |

    21|| `/gsd forensics` | Full debugger for auto-mode failures |
    22|| `/gsd cleanup` | Clean up state files and stale worktrees |
    23|| `/gsd visualize` | Open workflow visualizer |
    24|| `/gsd export --html` | Generate HTML report for current milestone |

    26|| `/gsd update` | Update GSD to the latest version |
    27|| `/gsd knowledge` | Add persistent project knowledge |
    28|| `/gsd fast` | Toggle service tier for supported models |
    29|| `/gsd rate` | Rate last unit's model tier (over/ok/under) |
    30|| `/gsd changelog` | Show release notes |
    31|| `/gsd logs` | Browse activity and debug logs |
    32|| `/gsd remote` | Control remote auto-mode |
    33|| `/gsd help` | Show all available commands |
    34|
    35|## Configuration & Diagnostics
    36|
    37|| Command | Description |
    38||---------|-------------|

    40|| `/gsd mode` | Switch workflow mode (solo/team) |

    42|| `/gsd keys` | API key manager |
    43|| `/gsd doctor` | Runtime health checks with auto-fix |
    44|| `/gsd inspect` | Show database diagnostics |
    45|| `/gsd init` | Project init wizard |
    46|| `/gsd setup` | Global setup status |
    47|| `/gsd skill-health` | Skill lifecycle dashboard |
    48|| `/gsd hooks` | Show configured hooks |
    49|| `/gsd migrate` | Migrate v1 `.planning` to `.gsd` format |
    50|
    51|## Milestone Management
    52|
    53|| Command | Description |
    54||---------|-------------|

    62|
    63|## Parallel Orchestration
    64|
    65|| Command | Description |
    66||---------|-------------|
    67|| `/gsd parallel start` | Analyze and start parallel workers |
    68|| `/gsd parallel status` | Show worker state and progress |

    73|
    74|## Workflow Templates

    82|
    83|## Custom Workflows
    84|
    85|| Command | Description |
    86||---------|-------------|

    91|| `/gsd workflow pause` | Pause workflow auto-mode |
    92|| `/gsd workflow resume` | Resume paused workflow |
    93|
    94|## Extensions

{"content": "     1|# Parallel Orchestration
     2|
     3|Run multiple milestones simultaneously in isolated git worktrees. Each milestone gets its own worker process, branch, and context window.
     4|
     5|{% hint style="info" %}
     6|Parallel mode is off by default. Enable it in preferences to use `/gsd parallel` commands.
     7|{% endhint %}
     8|
     9|## Quick Start
    10|
    11|1. Enable parallel mode:
    12|   ```yaml
    13|   parallel:
    14|     enabled: true
    15|     max_workers: 2
    16|   ```
    17|
    18|2. Start parallel execution:
    19|   ```
    20|   /gsd parallel start
    21|   ```
    22|   GSD scans milestones, checks dependencies and file overlap, shows an eligibility report, and spawns workers.
    23|
    24|3. Monitor:
    25|   ```
    26|   /gsd parallel status
    27|   ```
    28|
    29|4. Stop:
    30|   ```
    31|   /gsd parallel stop
    32|   ```
    33|
    34|## How It Works
    35|
    36|Each worker is a separate GSD process with complete isolation:
    37|
    38|| Resource | Isolation |
    39||----------|----------|
    40|| Filesystem | Own git worktree |
    41|| Git branch | `milestone/<MID>` |
    42|| Context window | Separate process |
    43|| Metrics | Own `metrics.json` |
    44|| Crash recovery | Own `auto.lock` |
    45|
    46|Workers communicate with the coordinator through file-based IPC — heartbeat files and signal files in `.gsd/parallel/`.
    47|
    48|## Eligibility
    49|
    50|Before starting, GSD checks which milestones can run concurrently:
    51|
    52|1. **Not complete** — finished milestones are skipped
    53|2. **Dependencies satisfied** — all `dependsOn` entries must be complete
    54|3. **File overlap** — milestones touching the same files get a warning (but are still eligible since they run in separate worktrees)
    55|
    56|## Configuration
    57|
    58|```yaml
    59|parallel:
    60|  enabled: false            # master toggle (default: false)
    61|  max_workers: 2            # concurrent workers (1-4)
    62|  budget_ceiling: 50.00     # aggregate cost limit
    63|  merge_strategy: "per-milestone"  # when to merge back
    64|  auto_merge: "confirm"     # "auto", "confirm", or "manual"
    65|```
    66|
    67|## Commands
    68|
    69|| Command | Description |
    70||---------|-------------|
    71|| `/gsd parallel start` | Analyze and start workers |
    72|| `/gsd parallel status` | Show all workers with progress and cost |
    73|| `/gsd parallel stop [MID]` | Stop all or a specific worker |
    74|| `/gsd parallel pause [MID]` | Pause all or a specific worker |
    75|| `/gsd parallel resume [MID]` | Resume paused workers |
    76|| `/gsd parallel merge [MID]` | Merge completed milestones to main |
    77|
    78|## Merge Reconciliation
    79|
    80|When milestones complete, their changes merge back to main:
    81|
    82|- `.gsd/` state files are auto-resolved
    83|- Code conflicts halt the merge — resolve manually and retry with `/gsd parallel merge <MID>`
    84|
    85|## Budget Management
    86|
    87|When `budget_ceiling` is set, aggregate cost across all workers is tracked. When the ceiling is reached, workers are signaled to stop.
    88|
    89|## Troubleshooting
    90|
    91|| Problem | Fix |
    92||---------|-----|
    93|| "Parallel mode is not enabled" | Set `parallel.enabled: true` |
    94|| "No eligible milestones" | All milestones are complete or blocked; check `/gsd queue` |
    95|| Worker crashed | Run `/gsd doctor --fix`, then `/gsd parallel start` |
    96|| Merge conflicts | Resolve in `.gsd/worktrees/<MID>/`, then `/gsd parallel merge <MID>` |
    97|| Workers seem stuck | Check if budget ceiling was reached via `/gsd parallel status` |
    98|", "total_lines": 97, "file_size": 3078, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Skills
     2|
     3|Skills are specialized instruction sets that GSD loads when the task matches. They provide domain-specific guidance — coding patterns, framework idioms, testing strategies, and tool usage.
     4|
     5|Skills follow the open [Agent Skills standard](https://agentskills.io/) and work across multiple AI agents, not just GSD.
     6|
     7|## Skill Directories
     8|
     9|| Location | Scope | Description |
    10||----------|-------|------------|
    11|| `~/.agents/skills/` | Global | Shared across all projects |
    12|| `.agents/skills/` (project root) | Project | Project-specific, committable to git |
    13|
    14|Global skills take precedence when names collide.
    15|
    16|## Installing Skills
    17|
    18|Skills are installed via the [skills.sh CLI](https://skills.sh):
    19|
    20|```bash
    21|# Interactive — choose skills and target agents
    22|npx skills add dpearson2699/swift-ios-skills
    23|
    24|# Install specific skills
    25|npx skills add dpearson2699/swift-ios-skills --skill swift-concurrency --skill swiftui-patterns -y
    26|
    27|# Install all from a repo
    28|npx skills add dpearson2699/swift-ios-skills --all
    29|
    30|# Check for updates
    31|npx skills check
    32|
    33|# Update installed skills
    34|npx skills update
    35|```
    36|
    37|## Onboarding Catalog
    38|
    39|During `gsd init`, GSD detects your project's tech stack and recommends relevant skill packs:
    40|
    41|- **Swift** — SwiftUI, Swift Core, concurrency, Charts, Testing
    42|- **iOS** — App Intents, Widgets, StoreKit, MapKit, Core ML, Vision, accessibility
    43|- **Web** — React, React Native, frontend design, accessibility
    44|- **Languages** — Rust, Python, Go patterns and best practices
    45|- **General** — Document handling (PDF, DOCX, XLSX)
    46|
    47|## Skill Discovery
    48|
    49|The `skill_discovery` preference controls how GSD finds skills during auto mode:
    50|
    51|| Mode | Behavior |
    52||------|----------|
    53|| `auto` | Skills found and applied automatically |
    54|| `suggest` | Skills identified but require confirmation (default) |
    55|| `off` | No skill discovery |
    56|
    57|## Skill Preferences
    58|
    59|Control which skills are used:
    60|
    61|```yaml
    62|always_use_skills:
    63|  - debug-like-expert
    64|prefer_skills:
    65|  - frontend-design
    66|avoid_skills:
    67|  - security-docker
    68|skill_rules:
    69|  - when: task involves authentication
    70|    use: [clerk]
    71|  - when: frontend styling work
    72|    prefer: [frontend-design]
    73|```
    74|
    75|## Creating Custom Skills
    76|
    77|Create your own skill by adding a directory with a `SKILL.md` file:
    78|
    79|```
    80|~/.agents/skills/my-skill/
    81|  SKILL.md           — instructions for the AI
    82|  references/        — optional reference files
    83|```
    84|
    85|The `SKILL.md` contains instructions the AI follows when the skill is active.
    86|
    87|### Project-Local Skills
    88|
    89|Place skills in your project root for project-specific guidance:
    90|
    91|```
    92|.agents/skills/my-project-skill/
    93|  SKILL.md
    94|```
    95|
    96|Project-local skills can be committed to git so team members share the same skill set.
    97|
    98|## Skill Health Dashboard
    99|
   100|Track skill performance:
   101|
   102|```
   103|/gsd skill-health              # overview table
   104|/gsd skill-health rust-core    # detailed view for one skill
   105|/gsd skill-health --stale 30   # skills unused for 30+ days
   106|/gsd skill-health --declining  # skills with falling success rates
   107|```
   108|
   109|The dashboard flags:
   110|- Success rate below 70% over the last 10 uses
   111|- Token usage rising 20%+ compared to previous window
   112|- Skills unused beyond the configured threshold
   113|
   114|### Staleness Detection
   115|
   116|```yaml
   117|skill_staleness_days: 60   # flag skills unused for 60+ days (0 to disable)
   118|```
   119|
   120|Stale skills are excluded from automatic matching but remain available for explicit use.
   121|", "total_lines": 120, "file_size": 3372, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Headless & CI Mode
     2|
     3|`gsd headless` runs GSD commands without a terminal UI — designed for CI pipelines, cron jobs, and scripted automation.
     4|
     5|## Basic Usage
     6|
     7|```bash
     8|# Run auto mode
     9|gsd headless
    10|
    11|# Run a single unit
    12|gsd headless next
    13|
    14|# With timeout for CI
    15|gsd headless --timeout 600000 auto
    16|
    17|# Force a specific phase
    18|gsd headless dispatch plan
    19|
    20|# Stream all events as JSONL
    21|gsd headless --json auto
    22|```
    23|
    24|## Creating Milestones Headlessly
    25|
    26|```bash
    27|# From a context file
    28|gsd headless new-milestone --context brief.md --auto
    29|
    30|# From inline text
    31|gsd headless new-milestone --context-text "Build a REST API with auth"
    32|
    33|# Pipe from stdin
    34|echo "Build a CLI tool" | gsd headless new-milestone --context -
    35|```
    36|
    37|## CLI Flags
    38|
    39|| Flag | Default | Description |
    40||------|---------|-------------|
    41|| `--timeout N` | 300000 (5 min) | Overall timeout in milliseconds |
    42|| `--max-restarts N` | 3 | Auto-restart on crash (0 to disable) |
    43|| `--json` | — | Stream events as JSONL to stdout |
    44|| `--model ID` | — | Override model for this session |
    45|| `--context <file>` | — | Context file for `new-milestone` (use `-` for stdin) |
    46|| `--context-text <text>` | — | Inline context for `new-milestone` |
    47|| `--auto` | — | Chain into auto mode after milestone creation |
    48|
    49|## Exit Codes
    50|
    51|| Code | Meaning |
    52||------|---------|
    53|| `0` | Complete |
    54|| `1` | Error or timeout |
    55|| `2` | Blocked |
    56|
    57|## Instant State Query
    58|
    59|`gsd headless query` returns a JSON snapshot of project state — no AI session, instant response (~50ms):
    60|
    61|```bash
    62|gsd headless query | jq '.state.phase'
    63|# "executing"
    64|
    65|gsd headless query | jq '.next'
    66|# {"action":"dispatch","unitType":"execute-task","unitId":"M001/S01/T03"}
    67|
    68|gsd headless query | jq '.cost.total'
    69|# 4.25
    70|```
    71|
    72|Any `/gsd` subcommand works as a positional argument: `gsd headless status`, `gsd headless doctor`, etc.
    73|
    74|## MCP Server Mode
    75|
    76|`gsd --mode mcp` runs GSD as a Model Context Protocol server over stdin/stdout, exposing all GSD tools to external AI clients:
    77|
    78|```bash
    79|gsd --mode mcp
    80|```
    81|
    82|Compatible with Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, and any MCP host.
    83|
    84|## Auto-Restart
    85|
    86|In headless mode, crashes trigger automatic restart with exponential backoff (5s → 10s → 30s cap, default 3 attempts). SIGINT/SIGTERM bypasses restart. Combined with crash recovery, this enables true overnight unattended execution.
    87|", "total_lines": 86, "file_size": 2327, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Migration from v1
     2|
     3|If you have projects with `.planning` directories from the original Get Shit Done (v1), you can migrate them to GSD-2's `.gsd` format.
     4|
     5|## Running the Migration
     6|
     7|```bash
     8|# From within the project directory
     9|/gsd migrate
    10|
    11|# Or specify a path
    12|/gsd migrate ~/projects/my-old-project
    13|```
    14|
    15|## What Gets Migrated
    16|
    17|The migration tool:
    18|
    19|- Parses your old `PROJECT.md`, `ROADMAP.md`, `REQUIREMENTS.md`, phase directories, plans, summaries, and research
    20|- Maps phases → slices, plans → tasks, milestones → milestones
    21|- Preserves completion state (`[x]` phases stay done, summaries carry over)
    22|- Consolidates research files into the new structure
    23|- Shows a preview before writing anything
    24|- Optionally runs an AI-driven review for quality assurance
    25|
    26|## Supported Formats
    27|
    28|The migration handles various v1 format variations:
    29|
    30|- Milestone-sectioned roadmaps with `<details>` blocks
    31|- Bold phase entries
    32|- Bullet-format requirements
    33|- Decimal phase numbering
    34|- Duplicate phase numbers across milestones
    35|
    36|## Requirements
    37|
    38|Migration works best with a `ROADMAP.md` file for milestone structure. Without one, milestones are inferred from the `phases/` directory.
    39|
    40|## Post-Migration
    41|
    42|After migrating, verify the output:
    43|
    44|```
    45|/gsd doctor
    46|```
    47|
    48|This checks `.gsd/` integrity and flags any structural issues.
    49|", "total_lines": 48, "file_size": 1312, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# GSD Workflow — Manual Bootstrap Protocol
     2|
     3|> This document teaches you how to operate the GSD planning methodology manually using files on disk.
     4|>
     5|> **When to read this:** At the start of any session working on GSD-managed work, or when loaded by `/gsd`.
     6|>
     7|> **After reading this, always read `.gsd/STATE.md` to find out what's next.**
     8|> If the milestone has a `M###-CONTEXT.md`, read that too. If the active slice has an `S##-CONTEXT.md`, read that as well — these files contain project-specific decisions, reference paths, and implementation guidance that this generic methodology doc does not.
     9|
    10|---
    11|
    12|## Quick Start: "What's next?"
    13|
    14|Read these files in order and act on what they say:
    15|
    16|1. **`.gsd/STATE.md`** — Where are we? What's the next action?
    17|2. **`.gsd/milestones/<active>/M###-ROADMAP.md`** — What's the plan? Which slices are done? (`STATE.md` tells you which milestone is active)
    18|3. **`.gsd/milestones/<active>/M###-CONTEXT.md`** — Milestone-level project decisions, reference paths, constraints. Read this before doing implementation work.
    19|4. If a slice is active and has one, read **`S##-CONTEXT.md`** — Slice-specific decisions and constraints.
    20|5. If a slice is active, read its **`S##-PLAN.md`** — Which tasks exist? Which are done?
    21|6. If `.gsd/CODEBASE.md` exists, skim it for fast structural orientation before broad code exploration.
    22|7. If a task was interrupted, check for **`continue.md`** in the active slice directory — Resume from there.
    23|
    24|Then do the thing `STATE.md` says to do next.
    25|
    26|---
    27|
    28|## The Hierarchy
    29|
    30|```
    31|Milestone  →  a shippable version (4-10 slices)
    32|  Slice    →  one demoable vertical capability (1-7 tasks)
    33|    Task   →  one context-window-sized unit of work (fits in one session)
    34|```
    35|
    36|**The iron rule:** A task MUST fit in one context window. If it can't, it's two tasks.
    37|
    38|---
    39|
    40|## File Locations
    41|
    42|All artifacts live in `.gsd/` at the project root:
    43|
    44|```
    45|.gsd/
    46|  STATE.md                                  # Dashboard — always read first (derived cache; runtime, gitignored)
    47|  DECISIONS.md                              # Append-only decisions register
    48|  CODEBASE.md                               # Generated codebase map cache (auto-refreshed by GSD)
    49|  milestones/
    50|    M001/
    51|      M001-ROADMAP.md                       # Milestone plan (checkboxes = state)
    52|      M001-CONTEXT.md                       # Optional: user decisions from discuss phase
    53|      M001-RESEARCH.md                      # Optional: codebase/tech research
    54|      M001-SUMMARY.md                       # Milestone rollup (updated as slices complete)
    55|      slices/
    56|        S01/
    57|          S01-PLAN.md                       # Task decomposition for this slice
    58|          S01-CONTEXT.md                    # Optional: slice-level user decisions
    59|          S01-RESEARCH.md                   # Optional: slice-level research
    60|          S01-SUMMARY.md                    # Slice summary (written on completion)
    61|          S01-UAT.md                        # Non-blocking human test script (written on completion)
    62|          continue.md                       # Ephemeral: resume point if interrupted
    63|          tasks/
    64|            T01-PLAN.md                     # Individual task plan
    65|            T01-SUMMARY.md                  # Task summary with frontmatter
    66|```
    67|
    68|---
    69|
    70|## File Format Reference
    71|
    72|### `M###-ROADMAP.md`
    73|
    74|```markdown
    75|# M001: Title of the Milestone
    76|
    77|**Vision:** One paragraph describing what this milestone delivers.
    78|
    79|**Success Criteria:**
    80|- Observable outcome 1
    81|- Observable outcome 2
    82|
    83|---
    84|
    85|## Slices
    86|
    87|- [ ] **S01: Slice Title** `risk:low` `depends:[]`
    88|  > After this: what the user can demo when this slice is done.
    89|
    90|- [ ] **S02: Another Slice** `risk:medium` `depends:[S01]`
    91|  > After this: demo sentence.
    92|
    93|- [x] **S03: Completed Slice** `risk:low` `depends:[S01]`
    94|  > After this: demo sentence.
    95|```
    96|
    97|**Parsing rules:** `- [x]` = done, `- [ ]` = not done. The `risk:` and `depends:[]` tags are inline metadata parsed from the line. `depends:[]` lists slice IDs this slice requires to be complete first.
    98|
    99|**Boundary Map** (required section in M###-ROADMAP.md):
   100|
   101|After the slices section, include a `## Boundary Map` that shows what each slice produces and consumes:
   102|
   103|```markdown
   104|## Boundary Map
   105|
   106|### S01 → S02
   107|Produces:
   108|  types.ts → User, Session, AuthToken (interfaces)
   109|  auth.ts  → generateToken(), verifyToken(), refreshToken()
   110|
   111|Consumes: nothing (leaf node)
   112|
   113|### S02 → S03
   114|Produces:
   115|  api/auth/login.ts  → POST handler
   116|  api/auth/signup.ts → POST handler
   117|  middleware.ts       → authMiddleware()
   118|
   119|Consumes from S01:
   120|  auth.ts → generateToken(), verifyToken()
   121|```
   122|
   123|The boundary map is a **planning artifact** — not runnable code. It:
   124|- Forces upfront thinking about slice boundaries before implementation
   125|- Gives downstream slices a concrete target to code against
   126|- Enables deterministic verification that slices actually connect
   127|- Gets updated during slice planning if new interfaces emerge
   128|
   129|### `S##-PLAN.md` (slice-level)
   130|
   131|```markdown
   132|# S01: Slice Title
   133|
   134|**Goal:** What this slice achieves.
   135|**Demo:** What the user can see/do when this is done.
   136|
   137|## Must-Haves
   138|- Observable outcome 1 (used for verification)
   139|- Observable outcome 2
   140|
   141|## Tasks
   142|
   143|- [ ] **T01: Task Title**
   144|  Description of what this task does.
   145|  
   146|- [ ] **T02: Another Task**
   147|  Description.
   148|
   149|## Files Likely Touched
   150|- path/to/file.ts
   151|- path/to/another.ts
   152|```
   153|
   154|### `T##-PLAN.md` (task-level)
   155|
   156|```markdown
   157|# T01: Task Title
   158|
   159|**Slice:** S01
   160|**Milestone:** M001
   161|
   162|## Goal
   163|What this task accomplishes in one sentence.
   164|
   165|## Must-Haves
   166|
   167|### Truths
   168|Observable behaviors that must be true when this task is done:
   169|- "User can sign up with email and password"
   170|- "Login returns a JWT token"
   171|
   172|### Artifacts
   173|Files that must exist with real implementation (not stubs):
   174|- `src/lib/auth.ts` — JWT helpers (min 30 lines, exports: generateToken, verifyToken)
   175|- `src/app/api/auth/login/route.ts` — Login endpoint (exports: POST)
   176|
   177|### Key Links
   178|Critical wiring between artifacts:
   179|- `login/route.ts` → `auth.ts` via import of `generateToken`
   180|- `middleware.ts` → `auth.ts` via import of `verifyToken`
   181|
   182|## Steps
   183|1. First thing to do
   184|2. Second thing to do
   185|3. Third thing to do
   186|
   187|## Context
   188|- Relevant prior decisions or patterns to follow
   189|- Key files to read before starting
   190|```
   191|
   192|**Must-haves are what make verification mechanically checkable.** Truths are checked by running commands or reading output. Artifacts are checked by confirming files exist with real content. Key links are checked by confirming imports/references actually connect the pieces.

   199|**Active Milestone:** M001 — Title
   200|**Active Slice:** S02 — Slice Title

     2|name: gsd-orchestrator
     3|description: >
     4|  Build software products autonomously via GSD headless mode. Handles the full
     5|  lifecycle: write a spec, launch a build, poll for completion, handle blockers,
     6|  track costs, and verify the result. Use when asked to "build something",
     7|  "create a project", "run gsd", "check build status", or any task that
     8|  requires autonomous software development via subprocess.
     9|metadata:
    10|  openclaw:
    11|    requires:
    12|      bins: [gsd]
    13|    install:
    14|      kind: node
    15|      package: gsd-pi
    16|      bins: [gsd]
    17|---
    18|
    19|<objective>
    20|You are an autonomous agent that builds software by orchestrating GSD as a subprocess.
    21|GSD is a headless CLI that plans, codes, tests, and ships software from a spec.
    22|You control it via shell commands, exit codes, and JSON output — no SDK, no RPC.
    23|</objective>
    24|
    25|<mental_model>
    26|GSD headless is a subprocess you launch and monitor. Think of it like a junior developer
    27|you hand a spec to:
    28|
    29|1. You write the spec (what to build)
    30|2. You launch the build (`gsd headless ... new-milestone --context spec.md --auto`)
    31|3. You wait for it to finish (exit code tells you the outcome)
    32|4. You check the result (query state, inspect files, verify deliverables)
    33|5. If blocked, you intervene (steer, supply answers, or escalate)
    34|
    35|The subprocess handles all planning, coding, testing, and git commits internally.
    36|You never write application code yourself — GSD does that.
    37|</mental_model>
    38|
    39|<critical_rules>
    40|- **Flags before command.** `gsd headless [--flags] [command] [args]`. Flags after the command are ignored.
    41|- **Redirect stderr.** JSON output goes to stdout. Progress goes to stderr. Always `2>/dev/null` when parsing JSON.
    42|- **Check exit codes.** 0=success, 1=error, 10=blocked (needs you), 11=cancelled.
    43|- **Use `query` to poll.** Instant (~50ms), no LLM cost. Use it between steps, not `auto` for status.
    44|- **Budget awareness.** Track `cost.total` from query results. Set limits before launching long runs.
    45|- **One project directory per build.** Each GSD project needs its own directory with a `.gsd/` folder.
    46|</critical_rules>
    47|
    48|<routing>
    49|Route based on what you need to do:
    50|
    51|**Build something from scratch:**
    52|Read `workflows/build-from-spec.md` — write spec, init directory, launch, monitor, verify.
    53|
    54|**Check on a running or completed build:**
    55|Read `workflows/monitor-and-poll.md` — query state, interpret phases, handle blockers.
    56|
    57|**Execute with fine-grained control:**
    58|Read `workflows/step-by-step.md` — run one unit at a time with decision points.
    59|
    60|**Understand the JSON output:**
    61|Read `references/json-result.md` — field reference for HeadlessJsonResult.
    62|
    63|**Pre-supply answers or secrets:**
    64|Read `references/answer-injection.md` — answer file schema and injection mechanism.
    65|
    66|**Look up a specific command:**
    67|Read `references/commands.md` — full command reference with flags and examples.
    68|</routing>
    69|
    70|<quick_reference>
    71|
    72|**Launch a full build (spec to working code):**
    73|```bash
    74|mkdir -p /tmp/my-project && cd /tmp/my-project && git init
    75|cat > spec.md << 'EOF'
    76|# Your Product Spec Here
    77|Build a ...
    78|EOF
    79|gsd headless --output-format json --context spec.md new-milestone --auto 2>/dev/null
    80|```
    81|
    82|**Check project state (instant, free):**
    83|```bash
    84|cd /path/to/project
    85|gsd headless query | jq '{phase: .state.phase, progress: .state.progress, cost: .cost.total}'
    86|```
    87|
    88|**Resume work on an existing project:**
    89|```bash
    90|cd /path/to/project
    91|gsd headless --output-format json auto 2>/dev/null
    92|```
    93|
    94|**Run one step at a time:**
    95|```bash
    96|RESULT=$(gsd headless --output-format json next 2>/dev/null)
    97|echo "$RESULT" | jq '{status: .status, phase: .phase, cost: .cost.total}'
    98|```
    99|
   100|</quick_reference>
   101|
   102|<exit_codes>
   103|| Code | Meaning | Your action |
   104||------|---------|-------------|
   105|| `0`  | Success | Check deliverables, verify output, report completion |
   106|| `1`  | Error or timeout | Inspect stderr, check `.gsd/STATE.md`, retry or escalate |
   107|| `10` | Blocked | Query state for blocker details, steer around it or escalate to human |
   108|| `11` | Cancelled | Process was interrupted — resume with `--resume <sessionId>` or restart |
   109|</exit_codes>
   110|
   111|<project_structure>
   112|GSD creates and manages all state in `.gsd/`:
   113|```
   114|.gsd/
   115|  PROJECT.md          # What this project is
   116|  REQUIREMENTS.md     # Capability contract
   117|  DECISIONS.md        # Architectural decisions (append-only)
   118|  KNOWLEDGE.md        # Persistent project knowledge (patterns, rules, lessons)
   119|  STATE.md            # Current phase and next action
   120|  milestones/
   121|    M001-xxxxx/
   122|      M001-xxxxx-CONTEXT.md    # Scope, constraints, assumptions
   123|      M001-xxxxx-ROADMAP.md    # Slices with checkboxes
   124|      M001-xxxxx-SUMMARY.md    # Completion summary
   125|      slices/S01/
   126|        S01-PLAN.md            # Tasks
   127|        S01-SUMMARY.md         # Slice summary
   128|        tasks/
   129|          T01-PLAN.md          # Individual task spec
   130|          T01-SUMMARY.md       # Task completion summary
   131|```
   132|
   133|State is derived from files on disk — checkboxes in ROADMAP.md and PLAN.md are the source of truth for completion. You never need to edit these files. GSD manages them. But you can read them to understand progress.
   134|</project_structure>
   135|
   136|<flags>
   137|| Flag | Description |
   138||------|-------------|
   139|| `--output-format <fmt>` | `text` (default), `json` (structured result at exit), `stream-json` (JSONL events) |
   140|| `--json` | Alias for `--output-format stream-json` — JSONL event stream to stdout |
   141|| `--bare` | Skip CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, user settings, user skills. Use for CI/ecosystem runs. |
   142|| `--resume <id>` | Resume a prior headless session by its session ID |
   143|| `--timeout N` | Overall timeout in ms (default: 300000, use 0 to disable) |
   144|| `--model ID` | Override LLM model |
   145|| `--supervised` | Forward interactive UI requests to orchestrator via stdout/stdin |
   146|| `--response-timeout N` | Timeout (ms) for orchestrator response in supervised mode (default: 30000) |
   147|| `--answers <path>` | Pre-supply answers and secrets from JSON file |
   148|| `--events <types>` | Filter JSONL to specific event types (comma-separated, implies `--json`) |
   149|| `--verbose` | Show tool calls in progress output |
   150|| `--context <path>` | Spec file path for `new-milestone` (use `-` for stdin) |
   151|| `--context-text <text>` | Inline spec text for `new-milestone` |
   152|| `--auto` | Chain into auto-mode after `new-milestone` |
   153|</flags>
   154|
   155|<answer_injection>
   156|Pre-supply answers and secrets for fully autonomous runs:
   157|
   158|```bash
   159|gsd headless --answers answers.json --output-format json auto 2>/dev/null
   160|```
   161|
   162|```json
   163|{
   164|  "questions": { "question_id": "selected_option" },
   165|  "secrets": { "API_KEY": "***" },
   166|  "defaults": { "strategy": "first_option" }
   167|}
   168|```
   169|
   170|- **questions** — question ID to answer (string for single-select, string[] for multi-select)
   171|- **secrets** — env var to value, injected into child process environment
   172|- **defaults.strategy** — `"first_option"` (default) or `"cancel"` for unmatched questions
   173|
   174|See `references/answer-injection.md` for the full mechanism.
   175|</answer_injection>
   176|
   177|<event_streaming>
   178|For real-time monitoring, use JSONL event streaming:
   179|
   180|```bash
   181|gsd headless --json auto 2>/dev/null | while read -r line; do
   182|  TYPE=$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.type')
   183|  case "$TYPE" in
   184|    tool_execution_start) echo "Tool: $(echo "$line" | jq -r '.toolName')" ;;
   185|    extension_ui_request) echo "GSD: $(echo "$line" | jq -r '.message // .title // empty')" ;;
   186|    agent_end) echo "Session ended" ;;
   187|  esac
   188|done
   189|```
   190|
   191|Filter to specific events: `--events agent_end,execution_complete,extension_ui_request`
   192|
   193|Available types: `agent_start`, `agent_end`, `tool_execution_start`, `tool_execution_end`,
   194|`tool_execution_update`, `extension_ui_request`, `message_start`, `message_end`,
   195|`message_update`, `turn_start`, `turn_end`, `cost_update`, `execution_complete`.
   196|</event_streaming>
   197|
   198|<all_commands>
   199|| Command | Purpose |
   200||---------|---------|
   201|| `auto` | Run all queued units until milestone complete or blocked (default) |
   202|| `next` | Run exactly one unit, then exit |
   203|| `query` | Instant JSON snapshot — state, next dispatch, costs (no LLM, ~50ms) |
   204|| `new-milestone` | Create milestone from spec file |
   205|| `dispatch <phase>` | Force specific phase (research, plan, execute, complete, reassess, uat, replan) |
   206|| `stop` / `pause` | Control auto-mode |
   207|| `steer <desc>` | Hard-steer plan mid-execution |
   208|| `skip` / `undo` | Unit control |
   209|| `queue` | Queue/reorder milestones |
   210|| `history` | View execution history |
   211|| `doctor` | Health check + auto-fix |
   212|| `knowledge <rule>` | Add persistent project knowledge |
   213|
   214|See `references/commands.md` for the complete reference.
   215|</all_commands>

{"content": "     1|# Build From Spec
     2|
     3|End-to-end workflow: take a product idea or specification, produce working software.
     4|
     5|## Prerequisites
     6|
     7|- `gsd` CLI installed (`npm install -g gsd-pi`)
     8|- A directory for the project (can be empty)
     9|- Git initialized in the directory
    10|
    11|## Process
    12|
    13|### Step 1: Prepare the project directory
    14|
    15|```bash
    16|PROJECT_DIR="/tmp/my-project-name"
    17|mkdir -p "$PROJECT_DIR"
    18|cd "$PROJECT_DIR"
    19|git init 2>/dev/null  # GSD needs a git repo
    20|```
    21|
    22|### Step 2: Write the spec file
    23|
    24|Write a spec file that describes what to build. More detail = better results.
    25|
    26|```bash
    27|cat > spec.md << 'SPEC'
    28|# Product Name
    29|
    30|## What
    31|[Concrete description of what to build]
    32|
    33|## Requirements
    34|- [Specific, testable requirement 1]
    35|- [Specific, testable requirement 2]
    36|- [Specific, testable requirement 3]
    37|
    38|## Technical Constraints
    39|- [Language, framework, or platform requirements]
    40|- [External services or APIs involved]
    41|- [Performance or security requirements]
    42|
    43|## Out of Scope
    44|- [Things explicitly NOT included]
    45|SPEC
    46|```
    47|
    48|**Spec quality matters.** Vague specs produce vague results. Include:
    49|- What the user can DO when it's done (not what code to write)
    50|- Technical constraints (language, framework, Node version)
    51|- What's out of scope (prevents scope creep)
    52|
    53|### Step 3: Launch the build
    54|
    55|**Fire-and-forget (simplest — GSD does everything):**
    56|```bash
    57|cd "$PROJECT_DIR"
    58|RESULT=$(gsd headless --output-format json --timeout 0 --context spec.md new-milestone --auto 2>/dev/null)
    59|EXIT=$?
    60|```
    61|
    62|`--timeout 0` disables the timeout for long builds. `--auto` chains milestone creation into execution.
    63|
    64|**With budget limit:**
    65|```bash
    66|# Use step-by-step mode with budget checks instead of auto
    67|# See workflows/step-by-step.md
    68|```
    69|
    70|**For CI or ecosystem runs (no user config):**
    71|```bash
    72|RESULT=$(gsd headless --bare --output-format json --timeout 0 --context spec.md new-milestone --auto 2>/dev/null)
    73|EXIT=$?
    74|```
    75|
    76|### Step 4: Handle the result
    77|
    78|```bash
    79|case $EXIT in
    80|  0)
    81|    # Success — verify deliverables
    82|    STATUS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.status')
    83|    COST=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.cost.total')
    84|    COMMITS=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.commits | length')
    85|    echo "Build complete: $STATUS, cost: \$$COST, commits: $COMMITS"
    86|
    87|    # Inspect what was built
    88|    gsd headless query | jq '.state.progress'
    89|
    90|    # Check the actual files
    91|    ls -la "$PROJECT_DIR"
    92|    ;;
    93|  1)
    94|    # Error — inspect and decide
    95|    echo "Build failed"
    96|    echo "$RESULT" | jq '{status: .status, phase: .phase}'
    97|
    98|    # Check state for details
    99|    gsd headless query | jq '.state'
   100|    ;;
   101|  10)
   102|    # Blocked — needs intervention
   103|    echo "Build blocked — needs human input"
   104|    gsd headless query | jq '{phase: .state.phase, blockers: .state.blockers}'
   105|
   106|    # Options: steer, supply answers, or escalate
   107|    # See workflows/monitor-and-poll.md for blocker handling
   108|    ;;
   109|  11)
   110|    echo "Build was cancelled"
   111|    ;;
   112|esac
   113|```
   114|
   115|### Step 5: Verify deliverables
   116|
   117|After a successful build, verify the output:
   118|
   119|```bash
   120|cd "$PROJECT_DIR"
   121|
   122|# Check project state
   123|gsd headless query | jq '{
   124|  phase: .state.phase,
   125|  progress: .state.progress,
   126|  cost: .cost.total
   127|}'
   128|
   129|# Check git log for what was built
   130|git log --oneline
   131|
   132|# Run the project's own tests if they exist
   133|[ -f package.json ] && npm test 2>/dev/null
   134|[ -f Makefile ] && make test 2>/dev/null
   135|```
   136|
   137|## Complete Example
   138|
   139|```bash
   140|# 1. Setup
   141|mkdir -p /tmp/todo-api && cd /tmp/todo-api && git init
   142|
   143|# 2. Write spec
   144|cat > spec.md << 'SPEC'
   145|# Todo API
   146|
   147|Build a REST API for managing todo items using Node.js and Express.
   148|
   149|## Requirements
   150|- GET /todos — list all todos
   151|- POST /todos — create a todo (title, completed)
   152|- PUT /todos/:id — update a todo
   153|- DELETE /todos/:id — delete a todo
   154|- Todos stored in-memory (no database)
   155|- Input validation with descriptive error messages
   156|- Health check endpoint at GET /health
   157|
   158|## Technical Constraints
   159|- Node.js with ESM modules
   160|- Express framework
   161|- No external database — in-memory array
   162|- Port configurable via PORT env var (default 3000)
   163|
   164|## Out of Scope
   165|- Authentication
   166|- Persistent storage
   167|- Frontend
   168|SPEC
   169|
   170|# 3. Launch
   171|RESULT=$(gsd headless --output-format json --timeout 0 --context spec.md new-milestone --auto 2>/dev/null)
   172|EXIT=$?
   173|
   174|# 4. Report
   175|if [ $EXIT -eq 0 ]; then
   176|  COST=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.cost.total')
   177|  echo "Build complete (\$$COST)"
   178|  echo "Files created:"
   179|  find . -not -path './.gsd/*' -not -path './.git/*' -type f
   180|else
   181|  echo "Build failed (exit $EXIT)"
   182|  echo "$RESULT" | jq .
   183|fi
   184|```

{"content": "     1|# MCP Servers
     2|
     3|GSD can connect to external MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers for local tools, internal APIs, self-hosted services, or integrations not built in as native extensions.
     4|
     5|## Configuration Files
     6|
     7|GSD reads MCP config from these project-local paths:
     8|
     9|- `.mcp.json` — repo-shared config (safe to commit)
    10|- `.gsd/mcp.json` — local-only config (not shared)
    11|
    12|If both exist, server names are merged and the first definition found wins.
    13|
    14|## Supported Transports
    15|
    16|| Transport | Config Shape | Use When |
    17||-----------|-------------|----------|
    18|| `stdio` | `command` + optional `args`, `env`, `cwd` | Launching a local MCP server |
    19|| `http` | `url` | Connecting to an already-running server |
    20|
    21|## Examples
    22|
    23|### stdio Server
    24|
    25|```json
    26|{
    27|  "mcpServers": {
    28|    "my-server": {
    29|      "type": "stdio",
    30|      "command": "/absolute/path/to/python3",
    31|      "args": ["/absolute/path/to/server.py"],
    32|      "env": {
    33|        "API_URL": "http://localhost:8000"
    34|      }
    35|    }
    36|  }
    37|}
    38|```
    39|
    40|### HTTP Server
    41|
    42|```json
    43|{
    44|  "mcpServers": {
    45|    "my-http-server": {
    46|      "url": "http://localhost:8080/mcp"
    47|    }
    48|  }
    49|}
    50|```
    51|
    52|## Verifying a Server
    53|
    54|After adding config, verify from a GSD session:
    55|
    56|1. `mcp_servers` — confirms GSD sees the config
    57|2. `mcp_discover(server="my-server")` — confirms the server starts and responds
    58|3. `mcp_call(server="my-server", tool="<tool>", args={...})` — confirms a real tool call works
    59|
    60|## Tips
    61|
    62|- Use **absolute paths** for executables and scripts
    63|- Set required **environment variables** directly in the MCP config's `env` block
    64|- Use `.mcp.json` for team-shared servers; `.gsd/mcp.json` for machine-local ones
    65|- If a server depends on local paths or personal secrets, keep it in `.gsd/mcp.json`
    66|", "total_lines": 65, "file_size": 1719, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Dynamic Model Routing
     2|
     3|Dynamic model routing automatically selects cheaper models for simple work and reserves expensive models for complex tasks. This reduces cost by 20-50% without sacrificing quality where it matters.
     4|
     5|## Enabling
     6|
     7|```yaml
     8|dynamic_routing:
     9|  enabled: true
    10|```
    11|
    12|## How It Works
    13|
    14|Each unit passes through two stages:
    15|
    16|1. **Complexity classification** — classifies work as light, standard, or heavy
    17|2. **Capability scoring** — within the tier, ranks models by how well they match the task
    18|
    19|**Key rule:** Your configured model is always the ceiling — routing never upgrades beyond what you've set.
    20|
    21|| Tier | Typical Work | Model Level |
    22||------|-------------|-------------|
    23|| Light | Slice completion, UAT, hooks | Haiku-class |
    24|| Standard | Research, planning, execution | Sonnet-class |
    25|| Heavy | Replanning, roadmap reassessment | Opus-class |
    26|
    27|## Configuration
    28|
    29|```yaml
    30|dynamic_routing:
    31|  enabled: true
    32|  tier_models:                    # optional: explicit model per tier
    33|    light: claude-haiku-4-5
    34|    standard: claude-sonnet-4-6
    35|    heavy: claude-opus-4-6
    36|  escalate_on_failure: true       # bump tier on failure (default)
    37|  budget_pressure: true           # auto-downgrade near budget ceiling (default)
    38|  cross_provider: true            # consider models from other providers (default)
    39|  capability_routing: true        # score models by task fit (default)
    40|```
    41|
    42|### Escalate on Failure
    43|
    44|When a task fails at a given tier, the router escalates to the next tier on retry: Light → Standard → Heavy. This prevents cheap models from burning retries on work that needs more reasoning.
    45|
    46|### Budget Pressure
    47|
    48|When approaching the budget ceiling, the router progressively downgrades:
    49|
    50|| Budget Used | Effect |
    51||------------|--------|
    52|| < 50% | No adjustment |
    53|| 50-75% | Standard → Light |
    54|| 75-90% | More aggressive |
    55|| > 90% | Nearly everything → Light |
    56|
    57|### Cross-Provider
    58|
    59|When enabled, the router may select models from providers other than your primary, using the built-in cost table to find the cheapest model at each tier.
    60|
    61|### Capability Routing
    62|
    63|Models are scored across 7 dimensions: coding, debugging, research, reasoning, speed, long context handling, and instruction following. Different task types weight these dimensions differently — a research task prioritizes research and reasoning, while an execution task prioritizes coding and instruction following.
    64|
    65|Set `capability_routing: false` to revert to simple cheapest-in-tier selection.
    66|
    67|## Interaction with Token Profiles
    68|
    69|Dynamic routing and token profiles work together:
    70|
    71|- **Token profiles** control phase skipping and context compression
    72|- **Dynamic routing** controls per-unit model selection
    73|
    74|The `budget` profile + dynamic routing provides maximum cost savings.
    75|
    76|## Adaptive Learning
    77|
    78|GSD tracks routing outcomes in `.gsd/routing-history.json`. If a tier's failure rate exceeds 20% for a given task type, future classifications are bumped up.
    79|
    80|Use `/gsd rate` to submit feedback:
    81|
    82|```
    83|/gsd rate over    # too powerful — use cheaper next time
    84|/gsd rate ok      # just right
    85|/gsd rate under   # too weak — use stronger next time
    86|```
    87|
    88|Feedback is weighted 2x compared to automatic outcomes.
    89|", "total_lines": 88, "file_size": 3202, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|# Working in Teams
     2|
     3|GSD supports multi-user workflows where several developers work on the same repository concurrently.
     4|
     5|## Quick Setup
     6|
     7|The simplest way: set team mode in your project preferences.
     8|
     9|```yaml
    10|# .gsd/PREFERENCES.md (committed to git)
    11|---
    12|version: 1
    13|mode: team
    14|---
    15|```
    16|
    17|This enables unique milestone IDs, push branches, pre-merge checks, and other team-appropriate defaults in one setting.
    18|
    19|## What Team Mode Does
    20|
    21|| Setting | Effect |
    22||---------|--------|
    23|| `unique_milestone_ids` | IDs like `M001-eh88as` instead of `M001` — no collisions |
    24|| `git.push_branches` | Milestone branches are pushed to remote |
    25|| `git.pre_merge_check` | Validation runs before merging |
    26|
    27|You can override individual settings on top of `mode: team`.
    28|
    29|## Configure `.gitignore`
    30|
    31|Share planning artifacts while keeping runtime files local:
    32|
    33|```bash
    34|# Runtime files (per-developer, gitignore these)
    35|.gsd/auto.lock
    36|.gsd/completed-units.json
    37|.gsd/STATE.md
    38|.gsd/metrics.json
    39|.gsd/activity/
    40|.gsd/runtime/
    41|.gsd/worktrees/
    42|.gsd/milestones/**/continue.md
    43|.gsd/milestones/**/*-CONTINUE.md
    44|```
    45|
    46|**What gets shared** (committed to git):
    47|- `.gsd/PREFERENCES.md` — project preferences
    48|- `.gsd/PROJECT.md` — living project description
    49|- `.gsd/REQUIREMENTS.md` — requirement contract
    50|- `.gsd/DECISIONS.md` — architectural decisions
    51|- `.gsd/milestones/` — roadmaps, plans, summaries, research
    52|
    53|**What stays local** (gitignored):
    54|- Lock files, metrics, state, activity logs, worktrees
    55|
    56|## Commit the Config
    57|
    58|```bash
    59|git add .gsd/PREFERENCES.md
    60|git commit -m "chore: enable GSD team workflow"
    61|```
    62|
    63|## Keeping `.gsd/` Local
    64|
    65|For teams where only some members use GSD:
    66|
    67|```yaml
    68|git:
    69|  commit_docs: false
    70|```
    71|
    72|This gitignores `.gsd/` entirely. You get structured planning without affecting teammates.
    73|
    74|## Parallel Development
    75|
    76|Multiple developers can run auto mode simultaneously on different milestones. Each developer:
    77|
    78|- Gets their own worktree (`.gsd/worktrees/<MID>/`)
    79|- Works on a unique `milestone/<MID>` branch
    80|- Squash-merges to main independently
    81|
    82|Milestone dependencies can be declared:
    83|
    84|```yaml
    85|# In M00X-CONTEXT.md frontmatter
    86|---
    87|depends_on: [M001-eh88as]
    88|---
    89|```
    90|
    91|GSD enforces that dependent milestones complete before starting downstream work.
    92|", "total_lines": 91, "file_size": 2243, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
